TextENGNew: ``...Well, well? Those people just now, weren’t they...``
``Two of them had Diet member badges.``
changeReason: Old says 'They had National Diet badges' (implying all had them); new says 'Two of them had Diet member badges' matching JPN 議員バッヂが二人 which specifies exactly two.

TextENGNew: ``Then that would be the prefectural assemblyman and the city councilor from the Sonozaki family.``
changeReason: Old says 'prefectural assembly member Sonozaki and city councilor Sonozaki' as individual identities; new says 'the prefectural assemblyman and the city councilor from the Sonozaki family' better conveying JPN 県議と市議の園崎 that the Sonozaki family holds both seats.

TextENGNew: ``That’s interesting. Relatives holding both the prefectural and city council seats.``

``It’s underhanded, is what it is. They plaster each other’s names everywhere for pre-campaign promotion.@k@vS20/11/440700038. While one is up for election, the other holds a separate rally—effectively doubling their campaign efforts.@k@vS20/11/440700039. Openly.``
changeReason: Old says 'Shouting out each other's name during elections'; new says 'They plaster each other's names everywhere for pre-campaign promotion'; JPN 事前運動バンバン means aggressive pre-campaign activity.

TextENGNew: ``I don’t really get it, but isn’t that a violation of election law?``

``As long as it doesn’t constitute pre-campaign activity, political activities are unrestricted. ...Kuma-chan, you’ll have a tough time if you ever get assigned to an election task force.@k@vS20/11/440700041. At least brush up on election law, will you?``
changeReason: Old mistranslates: 'As long as they have no prior consultation'; new correctly says 'As long as it doesn't constitute pre-campaign activity' matching JPN 事前運動にならない限り.

TextENGNew: ``I’m not cut out for the Intellectual Crimes division. I’m too dumb. Heheheh...!``
The visitors had been Prefectural Assemblyman Sonozaki and City Councilor Sonozaki.
And... the village chief of Hinamizawa was there too.
...Every last one of them under the Sonozaki family’s thumb.@k...How unpleasant.
``The ones seeing them off were... the deputy chief and our division head.``
It clicked.
That evening, when the division head invited me out for oden, I knew exactly what was coming.
``Ooishi-san, you’ve got a lot of connections, so you may have already heard... have you?``
``No. Not a thing.``
``Ma’am, I’ll have a tofu fritter and a fish cake, please. ...The assemblymen stormed into the chief’s office in a rage.``
changeReason: Old says 'The councilors stormed in on the police chief in a rage'; new says 'The assemblymen stormed into the chief's office in a rage'; JPN 署長んとこに = 'at/into the chief's office,' which the new specifies.

TextENGNew: ``Oh my. Is that so? ...Ma’am, another round for me, please.``
Both Sonozakis—prefectural and city—were the intimidation type.
Being berated by those men who straddled the line between politician and yakuza would be more than enough to rattle our green, career-track police chief...
changeReason: Old definitively says 'Our snot-nosed career police chief couldn't handle being yelled at'; new uses speculative 'would be more than enough to rattle' matching JPN conditional キツイでしょうねぇ.

TextENGNew: ``They came with a formal complaint about the Hinamizawa case investigation—and they named you specifically.``
changeReason: Old says 'A petition with your name came up regarding...'; new says 'They came with a formal complaint...and they named you specifically'; JPN 君を指名して陳情 emphasizes being singled out by name.

TextENGNew: ``Goodness, me? Well, well.``
``Don’t play dumb. You’ve been dredging up the old Hinamizawa cases,@k@vS20/00/442700005. haven’t you?``
``I’ve got my hands full with the Tomitake murder. I don’t have the bandwidth for anything else. Nahahahah!``
changeReason: Old says 'I've already got my hands full with Tomitake's death'; new adds 'I don't have the bandwidth for anything else' matching JPN そんな余裕ないですよ (no capacity for that).

TextENGNew: ``Really? I hope that’s true for your sake...``
A period of silence.
We said nothing, wordlessly working our chopsticks and draining our beers.
``Well, thank you for the meal. This month’s been nothing but losses, so my wallet was hurting.@k@vS20/11/440700047. You’re a lifesaver.``
``Don’t mention it. Just give me another horse tip sometime.@k@vS20/00/442700008. I’ll bet on whatever you’re betting on.``
``Nahahahah! I’ve been terrible lately.@k@vS20/11/440700049. Can’t read the horses at all! ...Taxi!!!``
I would take the train.
The division head was taking a hired car.
changeReason: Old says 'department chief is taking a taxi'; new says 'division head was taking a hired car'; JPN ハイヤー specifically means hired car (higher-end than taxi), and 課長 is 'division head.'

TextENGNew: Driving my own car was out of the question.@kI couldn’t throw away my career right before retirement with a drunk driving charge.
changeReason: Old says 'I have my own car, but it'd be rash to drive it home'; new says 'Driving my own car was out of the question. I couldn't throw away my career right before retirement with a drunk driving charge.' New adds explicit reasoning matching JPN 退職前にして飲酒運転でパー.

TextENGNew: His tongue was still loose enough, but from the waist down, the division head was clearly done for.
I stuffed him into the taxi and gave the driver his home address.
``Well then, see you tomorrow. Take care...!``
``Ooishi-san.``
``Yeah, yeah?``
``The past cases have all been individually closed. Stop trying to connect them.@k@vS20/00/442700011. Those villagers half-believe it’s genuinely a curse, you know.``
``I don’t believe in any curse either.``
changeReason: Old translates 祟りなんか信じちゃいませんよ as 'I'm not convinced' which is ambiguous/potentially opposite; new correctly says 'I don't believe in any curse either.'

TextENGNew: ``You’re retiring next year, aren’t you?@k@vS20/00/442700013. Weren’t you going to pay off your mortgage with the severance and move up to Hokkaido with your mother?``

``The old lady keeps crying about wanting to go back to Hokkaido where she was born... It’s my last act of filial duty.@k@vS20/11/440700054. As for the severance—well, I’ll enjoy spending it in the entertainment district.@k@vS20/11/440700055. Nahahahah!!``
changeReason: Old says 'Mother pines to return to her birthplace'; new says 'The old lady keeps crying about wanting to go back to Hokkaido' -- JPN 婆さまが...帰りたいって泣く means she's crying/begging, conveying more emotional intensity.

TextENGNew: ``The chief mentioned he might reconsider your special retirement salary increase.``
A public servant’s retirement payout is calculated by multiplying their monthly salary at the time of retirement.
So, just before retirement, a special promotion of two pay grades—equivalent to two years’ worth—is granted to inflate the payout.@kThis sort of thing has become customary practice in these parts.
changeReason: Old says 'a special promotion of two pay grades'; new adds 'equivalent to two years' worth' matching JPN 二号給（２年分）which explicitly states the two-year equivalence.

TextENGNew: Of course, it’s hardly a commendable custom...
Incidentally, the difference two pay grades makes to the retirement payout is quite substantial.

``Our egghead career chief certainly knows how to make a model veiled threat. But, well...@k@vS20/11/440700057. Considering our salaries come from the taxpayers’ hard-earned money, I suppose it’s the way of the times. ...Hahahahah!``
changeReason: Old omits the laugh 'はっはっはっは' at the end of the line that new includes; also old says 'it's the way of the times, I suppose' without the laugh, while new includes '...Hahahahah!' preserving the character's deflection through humor.

TextENGNew: It was anything but funny, but I laughed it off anyway.
``I don’t think it’s particularly model behavior either. But you’ve served long and well enough to deserve every yen of that payout.@k@vS20/00/442700016. Personally, I really want you to get it.``
``Well, if I can get it, of course I’d want it. ...Nahahahah!``
``You’ll get it. As long as you act like a grown-up about this.``
``Sorry for keeping you waiting, Driver. Please go ahead.``
I shut the door firmly, cutting the division head’s conversation off a bit roughly.
He looked like he still had more to say, but he cracked a wry smile and waved. I waved back.
The taxi gradually picked up speed and was soon swallowed by the river of lights.
``Nahahahah! ...Well, this is a problem. I wonder if I’ll be able to pay off that mortgage...``
